research identifies novel association between antibiotic resistance climate change.
research presented this week's 29th European Congress Clinical Microbiology Infectious Diseases (ECCMID) Amsterdam, Netherlands April 2019), identifies novel association between antibiotic resistance climate change.
study conducted Institute Infection Control Infectious Diseases, University Medical Center Göttingen (UMG), Germany, collaboration with Hannover Medical School (MHH), Germany.
lead author Professor Simone Scheithauer UMG.
Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) threat across Europe with burdens mainly peaking around Mediterranean Basin.
Recently, association with climate gained increased attention, since resistance increased with increasing local temperatures USA.
This research investigated whether explanatory strength climate variables holds true region with diverse healthcare systems societies whether climate change dimension identified, using Europe case region.
researchers conducted 30-country observational study across Europe (see below list countries).
six-year prevalence carbapenem resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA), Klebsiella pneumoniae (CRKP), multiresistant Escherichia coli (MREC), Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) determined based data published European Centre Disease Prevention Control (ECDC).
Statistical analysis computer modelling were performed identify associations between seasonal temperature, including potential socioeconomic health system related confounders.
team found significant associations CRKP, MREC MRSA with warm-season mean temperature, which higher contribution MRSA variance than outpatient antimicrobial drug use.
Furthermore, CRPA significantly associated with warm-season change temperature.
authors conclude: "Our study identified novel association between climatic factors Europe.
These results reveal aspects: climatic factors significantly contribute prediction different types healthcare systems societies, while climate change might increase transmission, particular carbapenem resistance."
They add: "While these results remain hypothetical unknown causal association exists, future analysis climatic developments necessary determine whether potential climate change effects become stronger."
Materials provided European Society Clinical Microbiology Infectious Diseases.
